column1: include this information: Timeline Table: The Development of Belief in the Devil (950 BCE – 70 CE) Period / Date Key Events & Beliefs References / Citations / Quotes "First Temple Period (950–586 BCE)" "- Israelite religion (Yahwism) was polytheistic, with a pantheon of gods. - No concept of Satan as Yahweh's opponent. - Angels not named; lower-tier beings. - Disasters attributed to Yahweh or lesser gods." "- “Yahwism...was, like other Northwest Semitic religions, polytheistic” (Niehr 1995:71-72; Smith 2004:101-110). - “The place names and the personal names preserved from Iron Age I reveal the designations of at least five deities...” (Hess 2007:242). - Psalm 82:1-2, Psalm 89:6-8 (court of gods). - “None of the angels are mentioned by name during this period...”" "Religious Reforms (8th–7th c. BCE)" "- Hezekiah and Josiah attempt monolatric/henotheistic reforms. - Move towards worship of Yahweh alone. - Polytheistic practice persists." "- “This first monolatric reform was not very successful...” (Albertz 2009:383). - “Israel’s official religion changed from being polytheistic into one that can be classified as monolatric or henotheistic.” (Becking 2001:192)." "Babylonian Exile (586–539 BCE)" "- Destruction of Jerusalem and Temple. - Collapse of polytheistic pantheon; shift towards monotheism. - Yahweh seen as responsible for all events. - Suffering prompts theological crisis." "- “The Babylonian conquest...had an enormous impact on the Judean belief system. Their symbolic world suddenly collapsed.” - Lamentations 5:20-22 (trauma and questioning God). - “The Deuteronomists read their perspective into the earlier history of Israel, creating the impression that Israel’s religion was monotheistic from the start.”" "Second Temple Period (539 BCE–70 CE)" "- Emergence of Satan as a heavenly being in texts (Zechariah, Job, 1 Chronicles). - Satan not yet Yahweh’s opponent; a member of the divine court. - Increased angelology; angels named. - Development of dualistic thinking under Hellenistic influence. - Suffering explained via Satan/Devil/Belial/Lucifer." "- “There are only three books in the Old Testament in which the noun is used to refer exclusively to a heavenly being: Zechariah 3:1-2; Job 1:6-12; 2:1-7:1 Chronicles 21:1.” - “None of these books characterises ‘Satan’ as Yahweh’s opponent; he is merely a member of the heavenly court.” - “Believers...resorted to belief in Satan...as a way of making sense of their world.”" "Early Christianity (1st c. CE)" "- Satan/Devil becomes central to Christian theology. - Used to explain evil, temptation, and suffering. - Christianity “cannot do without this character.”" "- “True Christianity is stuck with the Devil...” (Carl Braaten, in Oldridge 2012:92). - “The devil has been part of the fabric of European life since the Middle Ages, and has accompanied all its major changes” (Muchembled 2003:1)."

Integrated Timeline Table: Yemo, Yama, Variants, and the Development of Satan/Ahriman (Devil) Beliefs
Key Points of Integration

Yemo/Yama Lineage: The primordial Indo-European myth of Yemo splits into Yama (Vedic India) and Yima (Iran), both rulers of the dead. In Buddhist and Tantric traditions, Yama evolves into Yamantaka, who conquers death.

Satan/Ahriman/Devil Development: The concept of a cosmic adversary emerges in Zoroastrianism (Ahriman) and is later adopted and transformed in Jewish and Christian traditions. In early Israelite religion, there is no devil figure; Satan emerges as a member of the divine court during the Second Temple period, only later becoming the personification of evil in Christianity123578.

St. Christopher as Yama-like Figure: Medieval legends sometimes depict St. Christopher as monstrous or dog-headed, resonating with the archetype of underworld guardians such as Yama, Anubis, and related figures6.

Yama/Yamantaka Variants: In Buddhist and Tantric iconography, Yama is the judge of the dead, while Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava) is the wrathful conqueror of Yama, depicted in multiple forms (e.g., Krishna Yamari, Rakta Yamari).
Summary Table: Cultural Evolution of Death and Devil Figures

This integrated timeline demonstrates the complex evolution and cross-cultural interplay between concepts of death, judgment, and cosmic evil, from the earliest Indo-European myths through to the crystallization of the Devil in Christian theology, with Yama and his variants paralleling and sometimes merging with adversarial or threshold guardian figures in other traditions1235678.

Below is a merged, comprehensive timeline table integrating the evolution of Yemo, Yama, and variants across cultures, alongside the development of Satan/Ahriman (Devil) and the inclusion of St. Christopher as a Yama-like figure. The table keeps all relevant columns: period/date, culture/region, key figures and their development, devil/adversary development, descriptions, and references/citations where applicable.
Additional Notes on Variants and Forms
Yama/Yama Raja: Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain god of death and judge of souls, often riding a buffalo and wielding a noose or mace.
Yamantaka/Vajrabhairava: Tibetan Buddhist wrathful deity, “destroyer of Yama,” with multiple iconographic forms (13-, 17-, 21-, 49-deity mandalas).
Ahriman/Satan: Zoroastrian principle of evil, later influencing Abrahamic Satan as cosmic adversary.
St. Christopher: Medieval Christian figure sometimes depicted with dog or monstrous head, interpreted as a psychopomp or threshold guardian, analogous to Yama and Anubis.
Summary: Cross-Cultural Evolution of Death and Devil Figures

This comprehensive table captures the complex evolution and interplay between death gods, judges of souls, cosmic adversaries, and psychopomp figures across Eurasian cultures and religious traditions, illustrating how the primordial Yemo myth branched into diverse, yet thematically linked, figures such as Yama, Yamantaka, Satan/Ahriman, and even St. Christopher.
